interactions.summary: Jiang answers the final Australia-ships question by saying China is frantically extracting resources and dumping goods because of strategic desperation rather than coherent long-term planning.
The host and Jiang close the interview with mutual thanks and a brief note that Jiang is studying Orthodoxy because he sees it as geopolitically important.
Jiang ends by saying the current year will be bad and the next year worse.
A final farewell is captured under an UNKNOWN speaker label.

speaker_notes.note: The final 'Bye bye' is almost certainly just the host or Jiang closing the call rather than a third speaker.

claims.claim: Jiang says Chinese ships in Australian waters reflect a broader Chinese push to extract resources as fast as possible and dump goods globally out of desperation and anxiety.
Jiang says this Chinese extraction-and-dumping behavior is not a good long-term strategy.
Jiang says he is trying to understand Orthodox religion better because he thinks it is crucial for geopolitics.
Jiang predicts that the current year will be bad and the following year will be worse.

signature_moments.moment: The roaming Chinese fleet is explained as a panic move by a regime that feels time running out.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the interview a final distilled image of China's global behavior under pressure.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang closes with a minimal two-step forecast instead of reassurance.
signature_moments.source_phrase: this year's going to be really bad. And then the year after it's going to be worse
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It functions as the interview's final compressed prognosis.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang ends the interview with a consecutive two-year forecast: the present year is already bad, and the next year deteriorates further.

uncertainty_notes.note: Jiang's account of why Chinese ships are present in Australian waters is interpretive and strategic rather than evidence-based within this closing packet.
